title: Anesthesia for Hepatic Disease V2 – MCQ
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What is the gold standard scoring system to assess the severity of hepatic disease and risk assessment?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Child-Pugh classification — Child-Pugh classification is the gold standard scoring system to assess the severity of hepatic disease and hence the risk assessment (Table 21.1).
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation

questions.question: The aim of preoperative evaluation in hepatic patients is to assess the severity of disease AND:

options.B: The damage caused to other organs by hepatic dysfunction

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. The damage caused to other organs by hepatic dysfunction — The aim is not only to assess severity but also to assess the damage caused to other organs by hepatic dysfunction: coagulopathy, renal damage (hepatorenal syndrome), pulmonary dysfunction (hepatopulmonary syndrome), portopulmonary hypertension, encephalopathy, ascites, cardiac functions, electrolyte and metabolic abnormalities.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation

questions.question: In Child-Pugh classification, serum bilirubin value for Group C is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. > 3 mg% — In Child-Pugh classification: Group A: bilirubin < 2, Group B: 2–3, Group C: > 3 mg%.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation (Table 21.1)

questions.question: In Child-Pugh classification, serum albumin for Group A is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. > 3.5 gm% — Child-Pugh Group A: albumin > 3.5, Group B: 3–3.5, Group C: < 3 gm%.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation (Table 21.1)

questions.question: Child-Pugh Class C has a mortality of:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. > 50% — Per Table 21.1: Group A mortality 2–5%, Group B 10%, Group C > 50%.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation (Table 21.1)

questions.question: Hepatic patients should always be screened for:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hepatitis B and C — Always screen the hepatic patients for hepatitis B and C. Universal precautions must be taken intraoperatively if the patient is positive.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation

questions.question: When should elective surgery be deferred in acute liver disease?

options.A: For a fixed period of 6 weeks
options.B: Till the liver functions are stabilized

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Till the liver functions are stabilized — Elective surgery should be deferred in acute liver disease till the liver functions are stabilized. No need to defer in asymptomatic patients with mildly elevated liver enzymes.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Timing of Surgery

questions.question: Patients with moderate to severe chronic hepatic disease should have restoration of coagulation abnormalities by:

options.B: Vitamin K, cryoprecipitate or fresh frozen plasma

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Vitamin K, cryoprecipitate or fresh frozen plasma — Restoration of coagulation abnormalities (by vitamin K, cryoprecipitate or fresh frozen plasma) is essential before surgery.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Timing of Surgery

questions.question: Premedication with benzodiazepines in hepatic patients:

options.B: Should not be given as hepatic patients are oversensitive to sedative effects
options.C: Can be given in full dose

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Should not be given as hepatic patients are oversensitive to sedative effects — As hepatic patients may be oversensitive to the sedative effects of benzodiazepine, premedication with benzodiazepines should not be given.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Premedication

questions.question: Why is central venous pressure (CVP) monitoring highly recommended in hepatic patients?

options.B: Due to fluid loss to 3rd space and intravascular volume depletion

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Due to fluid loss to 3rd space and intravascular volume depletion — As there is fluid loss to 3rd space, the intravascular volume is depleted in these patients therefore, central venous pressure monitoring is highly recommended.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Monitoring

questions.question: Due to high volume of distribution in hepatic patients, the initial dose of intravenous agents is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. More (higher than normal) — Due to high volume of distribution the initial dose of intravenous agents is more while due to decreased metabolism the action of repeated/maintenance doses may be prolonged.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – General Anesthesia

questions.question: In hepatic patients, repeated/maintenance doses of IV agents may be prolonged because of:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Decreased metabolism — While initial doses are higher (high Vd), the action of repeated/maintenance doses may be prolonged due to decreased metabolism. Also decreased albumin may increase the unbound fraction of drugs.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – General Anesthesia

questions.question: The induction agent of choice for hepatic patients is Propofol because:

options.B: It has short half-life and extrahepatic metabolism
options.C: It is completely metabolized by the liver

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. It has short half-life and extrahepatic metabolism — Propofol, due to its short half-life and extrahepatic metabolism, is the induction agent of choice for hepatic patients.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Induction

questions.question: The inhalational agent of choice for hepatic patients is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Sevoflurane — Sevoflurane is the inhalational agent of choice for two reasons: (1) Maximum perseverance of hepatic blood flow, (2) It does not produce Trifluoroacetic acid avoiding any possibility of hepatitis.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Maintenance

questions.question: The most important goal during maintenance of anesthesia in hepatic patients is:

options.B: Maximum perseverance of hepatic blood flow

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Maximum perseverance of hepatic blood flow — Maintaining hepatic blood flow should be the most important goal in these patients.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Maintenance

questions.question: Halothane must not be used in hepatic patients for TWO reasons:

options.B: May cause hepatitis AND causes significant decrease in hepatic and portal blood flow

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. May cause hepatitis AND causes significant decrease in hepatic and portal blood flow — Halothane must not be used due to two reasons: firstly, it may cause hepatitis and secondly it causes significant decrease in hepatic as well as portal blood flow.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Maintenance

questions.question: Nitrous oxide should be avoided in hepatic patients for TWO reasons:

options.B: It can increase pulmonary artery pressure (portopulmonary hypertension) AND requires higher O2 concentration (hepatopulmonary syndrome)
options.C: It causes renal failure and coagulopathy

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. It can increase pulmonary artery pressure (portopulmonary hypertension) AND requires higher O2 concentration (hepatopulmonary syndrome) — Nitrous oxide should be avoided for two reasons: it can increase pulmonary artery pressure (portopulmonary hypertension) and it allows only lower oxygen concentration when higher concentration is needed due to significant right to left shunt (hepatopulmonary syndrome).
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Maintenance

questions.question: Why are desflurane and isoflurane considered good alternatives to sevoflurane in hepatic patients?

options.B: They preserve hepatic blood flow and produce minimal trifluoroacetic acid (hepatotoxicity is just theoretical)
options.C: They have the strongest analgesic properties
options.D: They do not require a vaporizer

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. They preserve hepatic blood flow and produce minimal trifluoroacetic acid (hepatotoxicity is just theoretical) — Desflurane (least metabolism) and isoflurane preserve hepatic blood flow. The amount of trifluoroacetic acid produced is too minimal that hepatotoxicity appears to be just theoretical.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Maintenance

questions.question: Succinylcholine can produce prolonged block in hepatic patients because of:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Decreased pseudocholinesterase — Succinylcholine can produce prolonged block due to decreased pseudocholinesterase synthesized by the liver.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Muscle Relaxants

questions.question: The muscle relaxants of choice for hepatic patients are:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Atracurium and Cis-atracurium — As atracurium and cis-atracurium are metabolized by Hoffman degradation, they are the muscle relaxants of choice for hepatic patients (organ-independent metabolism).
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Muscle Relaxants

questions.question: Atracurium and Cis-atracurium are preferred in hepatic patients because they are metabolized by:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hoffman degradation — Hoffman degradation is organ-independent, making atracurium and cis-atracurium ideal for patients with hepatic dysfunction.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Muscle Relaxants

questions.question: Remifentanil is the opioid of choice in hepatic patients because:

options.B: It is metabolized by plasma esterases
options.C: It is excreted unchanged by kidneys

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. It is metabolized by plasma esterases — Remifentanil is the opioid of choice because it is metabolized by plasma esterases, independent of hepatic function.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Muscle Relaxants

questions.question: In hepatic patients, decreased metabolism of citrate (from blood transfusion) means these patients should receive:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Calcium after blood transfusion — As there is decreased metabolism of citrate (which can chelate calcium), these patients should receive calcium after blood transfusion.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT acceptable intraoperatively in hepatic patients?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hypotension — Hypotension can severely impair hepatic blood flow and is not acceptable intraoperatively. Other unacceptable conditions: alkalosis, hypoglycemia, sepsis, decreased urinary output.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Not Acceptable Intraoperatively

questions.question: Albumin is the preferred colloid for hepatic patients because hypotension:

options.B: Can severely impair hepatic blood flow

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Can severely impair hepatic blood flow — Hypotension can severely impair hepatic blood flow. Albumin is the preferred colloid for a patient with compromised hepatic functions.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Not Acceptable Intraoperatively

questions.question: Alkalosis is dangerous in hepatic patients because it:

options.B: Increases the conversion of ammonium to ammonia which crosses blood brain barrier to precipitate hepatic encephalopathy

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Increases the conversion of ammonium to ammonia which crosses blood brain barrier to precipitate hepatic encephalopathy — Alkalosis increases conversion of ammonium to ammonia which crosses blood brain barrier to precipitate hepatic encephalopathy.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Not Acceptable Intraoperatively

questions.question: Hepatic disease patients are prone for hypoglycemia because:

options.B: They have impaired gluconeogenesis and exhausted glucose stores

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. They have impaired gluconeogenesis and exhausted glucose stores — Hepatic disease patients have impaired gluconeogenesis and exhausted glucose stores therefore, they are prone for hypoglycemia.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Not Acceptable Intraoperatively

questions.question: Sepsis in hepatic patients is dangerous because it can precipitate:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hepatic encephalopathy — Sepsis can precipitate encephalopathy in hepatic patients. It is one of the conditions not acceptable intraoperatively.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Not Acceptable Intraoperatively

questions.question: Decreased urinary output in hepatic patients can precipitate:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hepatorenal syndrome — Decreased urinary output can precipitate hepatorenal syndrome in hepatic patients and is not acceptable intraoperatively.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Not Acceptable Intraoperatively

questions.question: Postoperatively in hepatic patients, which functions should be closely monitored?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Renal functions and urine output — Postoperatively, renal functions and urine output should be monitored along with good antibiotic cover and oxygenation.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Postoperatively

questions.question: Cirrhotic patients have intrapulmonary shunt, therefore postoperatively they are more prone for:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hypoxia — Cirrhotic patients have intrapulmonary shunt therefore, are more prone for hypoxia. Oxygenation should be closely monitored postoperatively.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Postoperatively – Oxygenation

questions.question: Due to existing coagulopathy and intravascular volume depletion, which anesthetic technique should be avoided in hepatic patients?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Central neuraxial blocks — Due to the possibility of existing coagulopathy and intravascular volume depletion, central neuraxial blocks should be avoided in hepatic patients.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Choice of Anesthesia

questions.question: When can central neuraxial block be considered in hepatic patients?

options.B: If there is no associated coagulopathy and hypotension can be prevented

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. If there is no associated coagulopathy and hypotension can be prevented — If there is no associated coagulopathy, central neuraxial block can be considered if hypotension can be prevented.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Choice of Anesthesia

questions.question: Patients with biliary obstruction exhibit bradycardia because of:

options.B: Direct effect of bile salts on Sinoatrial (SA) node

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Direct effect of bile salts on Sinoatrial (SA) node — These patients exhibit bradycardia because of direct effect of bile salts on Sinoatrial (SA) node.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Just after the release of biliary obstruction, which drug should be given to wash out the bile salts?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Mannitol — Just after the release of obstruction, mannitol should be given to wash out the bile salts.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Vecuronium should not be used in patients with biliary obstruction because:

options.D: It is metabolized to toxic metabolites

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. It has 30–40% excretion through bile — Muscle relaxant vecuronium which has 30–40% excretion through bile should not be used in biliary obstruction patients.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Regarding opioids and sphincter of Oddi spasm, the textbook states that the incidence of opioid-induced spasm is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Less than 3% — The incidence of opioid-induced spasm of sphincter of Oddi is less than 3%, therefore strict guidelines of not using opioids for biliary colic has been questioned in current day practice.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: The strict guideline of not using opioids for biliary colic has been:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Questioned in current day practice — As the incidence of opioid-induced sphincter of Oddi spasm is less than 3%, strict guidelines of not using opioids for biliary colic has been questioned in current day practice.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: In Child-Pugh classification, prothrombin time prolongation from control for Group B is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. 4–6 seconds — Per Table 21.1: Group A: PT prolongation 1–4 sec, Group B: 4–6 sec, Group C: > 6 seconds.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation (Table 21.1)

questions.question: In Child-Pugh classification, ascites in Group A is:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. None — Per Table 21.1: Group A: No ascites, Group B: Moderate, Group C: Marked.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation (Table 21.1)

questions.question: Child-Pugh Group B has a surgical risk of:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Moderate — Per Table 21.1: Group A: Minimal surgical risk, Group B: Moderate, Group C: Marked.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation (Table 21.1)

questions.question: Why should sevoflurane be preferred over halothane — what metabolite does sevoflurane NOT produce?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Trifluoroacetic acid — Sevoflurane does not produce Trifluoroacetic acid on metabolism, thereby avoiding any possibility of hepatitis (unlike halothane).
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – Maintenance

questions.question: Encephalopathy in hepatic patients should be corrected preoperatively by:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Lactulose and neomycin — Correction of encephalopathy (by lactulose and neomycin) should be done before taking patients for surgery.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Timing of Surgery

questions.question: If ascites is significant enough to impair respiratory functions, what should be done preoperatively?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Tapping of ascites — Tapping of ascites (if significant enough to impair respiratory functions) should be done as part of preoperative optimization.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Timing of Surgery

questions.question: Child-Pugh classification includes all of the following parameters EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Creatinine — Child-Pugh includes: Bilirubin, Albumin, Prothrombin time, Ascites, Encephalopathy, Nutrition, Surgical risk, and Mortality. Creatinine is part of MELD score, not Child-Pugh.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation (Table 21.1)

questions.question: Decreased albumin in hepatic patients may increase the unbound fraction of which aspect of drug pharmacology?

options.B: Drug binding — increasing the free fraction of the drug

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Drug binding — increasing the free fraction of the drug — Decreased albumin may increase the unbound fraction of the drug, leading to exaggerated clinical effects of protein-bound drugs.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative – General Anesthesia

questions.question: Universal precautions intraoperatively in hepatic patients must be taken if the patient is positive for:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Hepatitis B or C — Universal precautions must be taken if the patient is positive for hepatitis B or C to protect the operating room staff.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Intraoperative

questions.question: Preoperative evaluation of hepatic patients should rule out all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: C. Rheumatoid arthritis — Important to rule out: coagulopathy, renal damage (hepatorenal syndrome), pulmonary dysfunction (hepatopulmonary syndrome), portopulmonary hypertension, encephalopathy, ascites, gastroesophageal varices, cardiac functions, electrolyte and metabolic abnormalities.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation

questions.question: In the management of biliary obstruction patients, what is stated about the general principles of hepatic disease management?

options.B: Whatever discussed for hepatic disease is applicable, with additional points to be taken care
options.C: Only regional anesthesia should be used

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Whatever discussed for hepatic disease is applicable, with additional points to be taken care — Whatever discussed for the management of patients with hepatic disease, the same is applicable for patients with biliary obstruction with additional points (bradycardia, mannitol, vecuronium avoidance, opioid caution).
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 200, Topic: Anesthesia for Patients with Biliary Obstruction

questions.question: Alcoholism should always be ruled out in hepatic patients because of associated:

options.B: Comorbidities including cardiomyopathy, pancreatitis, and nutritional deficiencies

questions.explanation: Correct Answer: B. Comorbidities including cardiomyopathy, pancreatitis, and nutritional deficiencies — Always rule out alcoholism and associated comorbidities in hepatic patients.
questions.reference: Ch 21, Page 199, Topic: Preoperative Evaluation
